coaching.coaching: Plan how concrete verification commands for AC5 and AC6 will be verified in-session — restate any deferred or unobservable success target as an observable proxy.
coaching.subject: concrete verification commands for AC5 and AC6

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the plan that the `_attach_code_review_metrics` re-export in gate_dispatch must be a top-level (eager) import — explicitly noting this as a deliberate exception to the all-lazy style, justified by the need for `gate_dispatch._attach_code_review_metrics` to resolve as a module attribute for the 7 test call sites.
evidence: Plan states: 'matching the file's all-lazy cross-module import style'
gate_dispatch.py lines 119-130, 595-608, 615-640, 648-679: every cross-package import is deferred inside a function body — there is no existing top-level cross-package import in the file

A top-level `from rebar.llm.code_review.finalize import ...` in gate_dispatch would eagerly load finalize.py + rebar.llm.code_review.__init__ (contracts + shim) on every `import gate_dispatch` call — inconsistent with the declared all-lazy style
findings.finding: The plan says the top-level re-export of `_attach_code_review_metrics` 'matches the file's all-lazy cross-module import style' — but a top-level `from rebar.llm.code_review.finalize import _attach_code_review_metrics` is NOT lazy; it contradicts the stated rationale and silently changes gate_dispatch's import behaviour (loading finalize.py + code_review/__init__ + contracts eagerly on every `import gate_dispatch`). The plan should either acknowledge this as a deliberate, justified exception to the all-lazy style (with a brief rationale) or specify a lazy re-export alternative (e.g. a `__getattr__` shim or an explicit note that the test-back-compat re-export is necessarily eager).
findings.location: Plan body, paragraph: 'Back-compat: gate_dispatch re-exports _attach_code_review_metrics (top-level import from finalize, # noqa: F401)'
scenarios: An implementer reads 'matching the all-lazy style' and attempts to make the re-export lazy (e.g. via __getattr__), which would break the 7-site `gate_dispatch._attach_code_review_metrics` attribute lookup in tests
The eager top-level import makes gate_dispatch heavier at import time — a latency/side-effect change the plan does not flag, leaving a future reader confused why this one import is not lazy
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence: 'This top-level import is a deliberate exception to gate_dispatch's all-lazy cross-module import style — it is necessary because the 7 test call sites reference `gate_dispatch._attach_code_review_metrics` as a module-level attribute, which requires the name to be bound at import time. The eager load of finalize.py is cheap (no heavy deps) and cycle-free per the analysis above.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the back-compat re-export rationale: gate_dispatch.py has no existing top-level intra-package imports — the proposed `from rebar.llm.code_review.finalize import _attach_code_review_metrics` at module level would break the lazy-import discipline; either use a lazy re-export (e.g., a module-level `__getattr__` shim or keep the function in gate_dispatch with a delegation stub) or explicitly acknowledge this is a deliberate style exception and verify it does not affect import-time behaviour.
evidence: gate_dispatch.py lines 26-33: top-level imports are only `from __future__ import annotations`, `from dataclasses import dataclass`, `from pathlib import Path`, `from typing import Any, NamedTuple`, `from rebar.llm.errors import LLMUnavailableError`
All other imports in gate_dispatch.py are lazy (inside function bodies): `from .runs import load_workflow_doc` (line 41), `from rebar.llm.config import gate_config` (line 121), etc.
The plan simultaneously says 'top-level import from finalize' AND 'matching the file's all-lazy cross-module import style' — these are mutually contradictory for intra-package imports
findings.finding: The plan asserts the top-level re-export of `_attach_code_review_metrics` in `gate_dispatch` 'match[es] the file's all-lazy cross-module import style,' but reading `gate_dispatch.py`'s actual top-level section (lines 26-33) shows only stdlib imports (`dataclass`, `Path`, `Any`, `NamedTuple`) and `LLMUnavailableError` from `rebar.llm.errors` at module level — every other cross-module import (`.runs`, `rebar.llm.config`, `rebar.llm.code_review.*`, etc.) is lazy inside function bodies. A top-level `from rebar.llm.code_review.finalize import _attach_code_review_metrics` would be the first eager intra-package cross-module import in this file, directly contradicting the stated rationale.
findings.location: Plan body — 'Back-compat' paragraph: 'gate_dispatch re-exports _attach_code_review_metrics (top-level import from finalize, # noqa: F401) … The top-level re-export is verified cycle-free … matching the file's all-lazy cross-module import style'
scenarios: An implementer following the plan literally will add the first eager intra-package module-level import to gate_dispatch.py, which breaks the lazy-import discipline stated elsewhere in the codebase
If the cycle-freedom argument depends on the lazy style, a top-level import changes the import-time dependency graph vs what the plan analyzed

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add explicit proving commands to AC5 and AC6: e.g. `grep -c gate_dispatch .github/module-size-allowlist.txt .github/module-size-ceilings.txt` (expect 0) and `python -c 'from rebar.llm.workflow import gate_dispatch; assert hasattr(gate_dispatch, "_attach_code_review_metrics")'`.
evidence: AC5: 'gate_dispatch.py removed from .github/module-size-allowlist.txt AND .github/module-size-ceilings.txt, and its remedy row deleted from the docs/architecture.md offenders table.' — no grep/command listed.
AC6: 'gate_dispatch._attach_code_review_metrics still resolves.' — no python -c command listed, unlike AC3 which provides one.
Contrast with AC3 which provides: python -c 'from rebar.llm.code_review.finalize import finalize_code_review_verdict'
findings.finding: AC5 ('gate_dispatch.py removed from allowlist/ceilings/architecture.md') and AC6 ('_attach_code_review_metrics re-exported') each state observable end-states but omit concrete proving commands, leaving the executor without an in-session command that produces evidence of success.
scenarios: An agent completing the work cannot produce CI-style evidence for AC5 and AC6 without inferring the commands, creating a verification gap.
findings.suggested_fix:
AC5 addition: `grep gate_dispatch .github/module-size-allowlist.txt .github/module-size-ceilings.txt docs/architecture.md` should return no matches.
AC6 addition: `python -c 'from rebar.llm.workflow import gate_dispatch; gate_dispatch._attach_code_review_metrics'` exits 0.

evidence: 2 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm that the three copies of `_LLM_STEP_KINDS` (gate_dispatch.py:217, runs.py:306, and the proposed finalize.py) are protected against silent drift — either add a one-line assertion test (`assert finalize._LLM_STEP_KINDS == gate_dispatch._LLM_STEP_KINDS`) or document why the mirror-comment alone is sufficient given the constant's historical stability.
evidence: gate_dispatch.py:217: `_LLM_STEP_KINDS = frozenset({"agent", "batch"})  # the billable LLM tier`
runs.py:306: `_LLM_STEP_KINDS = frozenset({"agent", "batch"})  # the billable, file-reading tool tiers`
Plan proposes a third copy in finalize.py, justified as avoiding a back-import cycle
No test asserting equality of the three copies is proposed or exists
findings.finding: The plan introduces a third in-process copy of `_LLM_STEP_KINDS = frozenset({'agent', 'batch'})` in finalize.py, with no drift-protection mechanism between the three copies (gate_dispatch.py:217, runs.py:306, finalize.py). If the workflow engine adds a step kind, two of the three copies would need manual updates with no automated signal of the mismatch.
findings.location: Plan body: 'finalize.py re-declares these as small local constants (_STEP_VERIFY = \'verify\', _STEP_DECIDE = \'decide\', _LLM_STEP_KINDS = frozenset({...})) with a mirror comment'
scenarios: A new workflow step kind (e.g., 'map') is added to the engine; gate_dispatch.py and runs.py are updated but finalize.py's local copy is silently missed, causing finalize.py to misclassify the new step kind's duration and omit it from llm_ms — a silent metrics regression with no test failure.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit (even one-sentence) rollback note to the plan: e.g. 'If any named test suite regresses post-merge, the back-out path is `git revert` of this commit; the re-export shim makes the revert safe with no consumer-side changes required.'
evidence: 'The top-level re-export is verified cycle-free' — plan asserts correctness but provides no back-out plan if the assertion is wrong.
'This is a pure/mechanical relocation' — the plan's confidence in the move implies rollback is not needed, but T4 requires explicit statement.
No section of the plan describes 'revert the commit' or an expand-contract phase sequence.
findings.finding: The plan provides no explicit rollback or back-out path for the migration. T4 requires that a reversibility mechanism be stated, not just that the breaking change is acknowledged. The only mitigation described is the re-export shim, which addresses forward compatibility but not what to do if the migration introduces an unforeseen import cycle or test regression post-merge.
findings.location: Back-compat section of plan body; Acceptance Criteria (AC7, AC8)
scenarios: An import cycle not caught by static analysis surfaces at runtime in an integration environment; without a stated rollback plan the team must improvise.
A test regression in test_code_review_gate_session.py is discovered after merge; the plan gives no guidance on whether to roll forward or revert.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a note to AGENTS.md (or the code_review subpackage README/design doc) documenting the mirror-constant pattern and the keep-in-sync rule, so future maintainers know the finalize.py private copies must track the gate_dispatch originals.
evidence: 'finalize.py re-declares these as small local constants (_STEP_VERIFY = \'verify\', _STEP_DECIDE = \'decide\', _LLM_STEP_KINDS = frozenset({...})) with a mirror comment naming the gate_dispatch originals'
ABSENCE: no AGENTS.md or design-doc update is specified to record the keep-in-sync rule for these mirrored constants
findings.finding: The mirror-constant pattern (re-declaring STEP_VERIFY, STEP_DECIDE, _LLM_STEP_KINDS as local private copies in finalize.py with only an inline comment naming the originals) is a novel maintenance contract with no entry in AGENTS.md or any design doc — future maintainers may silently let the copies drift if the gate_dispatch originals ever change value.
findings.location: Plan body — 'STRICT LEAF' section; new file src/rebar/llm/code_review/finalize.py
scenarios: A developer changes STEP_VERIFY or STEP_DECIDE value in gate_dispatch.py, doesn't know about the mirror copies, and introduces a silent behavioral divergence in finalize.py's metric tagging.
findings.suggested_fix: In AGENTS.md (or docs/architecture.md under the code_review subpackage entry), add a one-line rule: 'finalize.py mirrors _STEP_VERIFY/_STEP_DECIDE/_LLM_STEP_KINDS from gate_dispatch.py for cycle-breaking; if those values change, update finalize.py in the same commit.' Alternatively, consider a test assertion that both modules agree on the string values.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify test_code_review_ws5.py does not reference any of the moved symbols directly from gate_dispatch (or add it to the AC8 named passing-test list alongside the other code-review gate tests).
evidence: tests/unit/ directory listing shows test_code_review_ws5.py exists
The plan's AC8 enumerates only four named test files; test_code_review_ws5.py is absent
The code-review gate path is directly touched by this ticket; ws5 was explicitly added after ws4 for security-detector integration and may depend on moved symbols or gate internals
findings.finding: test_code_review_ws5.py exists in tests/unit/ but is not listed in the AC8 passing-test set, even though it likely exercises the code-review gate path. If it imports or monkeypatches any of the moved symbols directly (rather than through gate_dispatch), the split could silently break it without any AC catching the regression.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC8: 'tests/unit/test_code_review_fp_ledger.py, test_code_review_gate_session.py, test_code_review_ws4.py, test_gate_engine_cutover.py all pass'
scenarios: test_code_review_ws5.py directly references a moved symbol (e.g. imports _attach_code_review_metrics or STEP_ASSEMBLE_DIFF from gate_dispatch without going through the re-export), causing an ImportError or AttributeError that no AC checks.
The test passes only by accident (the re-export covers it) but the omission from AC8 means a future refactor that removes the re-export has no test oracle.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `test_code_review_ws5.py` to the AC8 list, or add a note explaining why it is explicitly excluded (e.g., 'ws5 exercises detectors only, not the finalize path').

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge tests/interfaces/store/test_snapshot_environments.py as an integration-path consumer of the moved success-branch logic (or confirm it is covered by the plan's 'existing test suites are the oracle' catch-all).
evidence: tests/interfaces/store/test_snapshot_environments.py:93: from rebar.llm.workflow import gate_dispatch
tests/interfaces/store/test_snapshot_environments.py:95: monkeypatch.setattr(gate_dispatch, 'code_review_enabled', lambda repo_root=None: True)
This test exercises the full code-review gate path including the moved success-branch body (finalize_code_review_verdict), yet is not mentioned in the plan
findings.finding: The plan does not enumerate test_llm_disposition_plumbing.py or tests/interfaces/store/test_snapshot_environments.py as gate_dispatch consumers — both import from gate_dispatch — even though neither moves symbols. The omission is benign for those specific imports, but test_snapshot_environments.py monkeypatches gate_dispatch.code_review_enabled and then exercises produce_code_review_verdict end-to-end, making it a de-facto integration test of the moved success-branch logic. A broken finalize.py would cause this test to fail with no AC covering it.
findings.location: Plan body: 'Back-compat: gate_dispatch re-exports _attach_code_review_metrics … load-bearing: tests/unit/test_code_review_fp_ledger.py … at 7 sites'
scenarios: finalize.py has a bug in the moved success-branch body; test_snapshot_environments.py catches it during its integration run, but the plan author did not expect this file to be a relevant oracle and may not investigate its failure.

findings.finding: The ticket has no `## Acceptance Criteria` checklist. A plan cannot be reviewed for completion without testable, checkable criteria.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an `## Acceptance Criteria` section with `- [ ]` checklist items, one per observable, in-session-verifiable outcome.

findings.finding: The plan specifies that finalize.py re-declares step-id literals named `_STEP_VERIFY/_STEP_DECIDE` (underscore-prefixed), but the actual constant names in gate_dispatch.py are `STEP_VERIFY` (line 56) and `STEP_DECIDE` (line 57) — public names with no underscore. The body of `_attach_code_review_metrics` being moved references `STEP_VERIFY` (line 795) and `STEP_DECIDE` (line 797); an implementer following the plan's naming spec literally would declare `_STEP_VERIFY = 'verify'` and `_STEP_DECIDE = 'decide'` as the local copies, causing an immediate NameError in the moved function unless the references are also renamed — which the plan prohibits ('no signature or logic changes').
findings.location: Plan body: 'The leaf is a STRICT LEAF: it re-declares the plan-review-shared step-id literals (_STEP_VERIFY/_STEP_DECIDE/_LLM_STEP_KINDS)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace '_STEP_VERIFY/_STEP_DECIDE/_LLM_STEP_KINDS' with 'STEP_VERIFY/STEP_DECIDE/_LLM_STEP_KINDS' in the plan's STRICT LEAF description. The local re-declarations in finalize.py should be `STEP_VERIFY = "verify"`, `STEP_DECIDE = "decide"`, `_LLM_STEP_KINDS = frozenset({"agent", "batch"})` — matching the actual names used by the moved function body.

findings.finding: The plan mislabels the step-id constants that finalize.py must re-declare as '_STEP_VERIFY/_STEP_DECIDE' (underscore-prefixed), but the actual names in gate_dispatch.py are 'STEP_VERIFY' and 'STEP_DECIDE' (public, no leading underscore, lines 56–57). An implementer following the plan would search for non-existent '_STEP_VERIFY'/'_STEP_DECIDE' names to copy into the leaf, potentially introducing a typo or naming divergence.
findings.location: Plan body: 'The leaf is a STRICT LEAF: it re-declares the plan-review-shared step-id literals (_STEP_VERIFY/_STEP_DECIDE/_LLM_STEP_KINDS) as small local constants'
findings.suggested_fix: Change '(_STEP_VERIFY/_STEP_DECIDE/_LLM_STEP_KINDS)' to '(STEP_VERIFY/STEP_DECIDE/_LLM_STEP_KINDS)' in the strict-leaf description, noting that the leaf's local copies should use the same public names (or rename them private with a mirror comment that shows the original name they shadow).

findings.finding: The plan refers to the re-declared local constants in finalize.py as '_STEP_VERIFY/_STEP_DECIDE' (private, underscore-prefixed), but the actual source symbols in gate_dispatch.py are STEP_VERIFY and STEP_DECIDE (public, no leading underscore). An implementer following the plan literally would name the finalize.py locals '_STEP_VERIFY'/'_STEP_DECIDE' while the mirror comment would need to say they shadow 'STEP_VERIFY'/'STEP_DECIDE' — a confusion hazard. The plan's naming should match reality.
findings.location: Plan body: 'The leaf is a STRICT LEAF: it re-declares the plan-review-shared step-id literals (_STEP_VERIFY/_STEP_DECIDE/_LLM_STEP_KINDS) as small local constants with a mirror comment'
findings.suggested_fix: Update plan text to: 're-declares the plan-review-shared step-id literals (STEP_VERIFY → local _STEP_VERIFY, STEP_DECIDE → local _STEP_DECIDE, _LLM_STEP_KINDS → local _LLM_STEP_KINDS) as small private local constants with a mirror comment naming the gate_dispatch originals'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the two conflicting import modes for `finalize` in `gate_dispatch`: either (a) keep the re-export top-level (breaking 'all-lazy' style, which is fine — just drop that rationale for the delegation import too) or (b) make the re-export lazy but also add a module-level `__getattr__` shim so `gate_dispatch._attach_code_review_metrics` resolves at attribute-access time; update the plan to state one consistent approach.
evidence: 'on success, lazily imports finalize and delegates (matching the file's all-lazy cross-module import style)'

'gate_dispatch re-exports _attach_code_review_metrics (top-level import from finalize, # noqa: F401) — load-bearing: tests/unit/test_code_review_fp_ledger.py calls gate_dispatch._attach_code_review_metrics at 7 sites'

If the re-export is top-level, finalize is already in gate_dispatch's module namespace at load time — the lazy import inside _run_code_review_gate serves no purpose and contradicts the all-lazy rationale.
findings.finding: The plan simultaneously instructs that _run_code_review_gate 'lazily imports finalize and delegates (matching the file's all-lazy cross-module import style)' AND that gate_dispatch does a 'top-level import from finalize, # noqa: F401' for the re-export. If finalize is already imported at the top level for re-export, the lazy import inside _run_code_review_gate is redundant and its stated rationale ('all-lazy style') is contradicted. Worse, an implementer who reads only the back-compat section and uses a top-level import everywhere satisfies AC6 but violates the stated style rationale; an implementer who reads only the approach section and makes the re-export lazy too satisfies style but breaks AC6 (gate_dispatch._attach_code_review_metrics won't resolve at module load time until _run_code_review_gate is called).
findings.location: Plan body: 'lazily imports finalize and delegates (matching the file's all-lazy cross-module import style)' vs. Back-compat section: 'gate_dispatch re-exports _attach_code_review_metrics (top-level import from finalize, # noqa: F401)'
scenarios: Implementer makes only the _run_code_review_gate delegation lazy (not the re-export) — fine for AC6 but the style claim is false.
Implementer makes the re-export lazy too (to honour 'all-lazy style') — AC6 fails because gate_dispatch._attach_code_review_metrics doesn't resolve at import time.
Implementer is confused and omits one or the other, introducing a silent regression.
findings.suggested_fix: Simplest resolution: keep the top-level re-export (it is load-bearing for tests), drop the 'matching all-lazy style' rationale for _run_code_review_gate's delegation (since finalize is already imported, just call it directly — no lazy import needed). Update the plan body to say '_run_code_review_gate calls finalize.finalize_code_review_verdict directly (finalize already imported at module top for re-export)'.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
